Now, let me show you how this actually works in practice because features are cool, but what matters is what you can do with them. Let's say you want to build a full automation system for the AI profit boardroom. Here's how you do it with clawed code step by step. Step one, you open clawed code and give it a clear prompt. something like create a node orjs API that takes a new member signup from our landing page, saves their info to a database, sends them a personalized welcome email, and adds them to our weekly AI tips email list. Claude Code writes the entire thing, server, roots, database connection, email logic, all of it. Step two, you use the code review feature. You tell Claude Code to review what it just built. It scans every file, finds any issues, maybe there's a missing error handler, maybe the email function doesn't retry on failure. It catches all of that and fixes it for you. Step three, you tell Claude Code to write tests. You say, "Write tests for the signup API to make sure it handles missing fields, duplicate emails, and server errors." It writes the tests, runs them. If something fails, it fixes the code and runs them again automatically. This loop keeps going until everything passes. Step four, you automate the deployment. You say create a deployment script that pushes this to production and sets up environment variables for the email service and database. Done. You just built, tested, reviewed, and deployed a full automation system. And you barely touched your keyboard. There have been real cases where AI tools have made mistakes, deleted files, overwritten important code. So, you always want to keep backups. Always review what it produces. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. Okay, so let me explain what Anthropic actually built. They created a brand new feature inside Claude Code called code review. And this is where it gets crazy. When you open a pull request on GitHub, Claude doesn't just look at your code like one reviewer would. It launches a whole team of AI agents, multiple agents, all working at the same time in parallel. Each agent is looking for something different. One agent is hunting for logic bugs. Another one is scanning for security risks. Another one is checking for performance problems. Another one is looking at architecture issues. And another one is searching for edge cases that could break things later. So instead of one set of eyes on your code, you've got a whole squad. And here's the part that really blew my mind. These agents don't just find stuff and dump it on you. They actually cross-check each other. They verify each other's findings. So the garbage gets filtered out, the false alarms get removed, and what you're left with is a clean, high signal report of real problems ranked by how serious they are. Then Claude posts the results right into your pull requests on GitHub. You get one clear summary comment at the top, plus inline comments on the exact lines that have problems. No noise, no fluff, just the stuff that actually matters. And the whole thing takes about 20 minutes. That's it. 20 minutes and your code has been reviewed by a team of AI agents. Now, here's where it gets even smarter. Claude doesn't treat every pull request the same. It actually scales up or down depending on how big and complex the code change is. So, if you've got a small simple change, it does a light review. But if you've got a massive pull request with hundreds of lines of code, it spins up more agents and does a deeper analysis. It adjusts automatically. You don't have to do anything. That is wild. Now, if you want to learn how to use AI tools like Claude Code to automate your workflows and scale your business, you need to check out the AI profit boardroom. It's the best place to learn how to save time with AI automation tools like Claw Code and get ahead of everyone else. I'll drop the link in the description and comments. Seriously, go check it out. Okay, so let me show you the results because this is where it gets really good. Before Anthropic turned on this feature, only 16% of their pull requests were getting a deep, thorough review. 16%. That means 84% of code changes were barely being checked. After they turned on Claude code review, that number jumped to 54%. More than triple. That's a massive jump in code quality. And it gets even crazier. For large pool requests, the ones with the most risk, Claude found issues in 84% of them. 84%. And on average, it was finding seven and a half problems per large pull request. These aren't fake warnings either. The false positive rate was less than 1%. Less than 1%. That means almost every single thing Claude flagged was a real problem that needed to be fixed. Now, let me tell you a story that really shows why this matters. There was a oneline code change. One line. It looked completely harmless. Any human reviewer would have glanced at it and said, "Yeah, that's fine." and moved on. But Claude flagged it as critical. And you want to think of Claw Code as a genius intern sitting inside your computer ready to code anything you ask for. Here's the catch. Claw code is powerful, but it has no structure. um no shortcuts, no specialized experts, no memory unless you add them in later, which is what we're doing. It's a raw brain with no playbook. And that's exactly what Superlaw fixes. Now, if you're wondering what Supergluid is, let me talk you through that. So, Supergluid is a free open source framework that sits on top of C code. It gives CL code 30/comands, so instant shortcuts, 16 AI agents, specialist um experts, seven behavioral modes, so different thinking styles, and eight MCP server integrations. These are plugin superpowers and superclaw basically transforms claw code into a structured development platform through behavioral instructions and component orchestration. Basically, it makes it a lot better. Core code is the engine. Supercloud is the steering wheel, GPS, and turbo boost. And it's built by over 52 contributors around the world. Has 21,000 stars and it's on version 4.2 right now. And it's 100% free on the MIT license. Now, if you're wondering how to install it, like I said before, you can just open up your terminal. Performs up to five um different trails of reasoning, scores every source on a credibility scale from 0 to one, tracks coverage completeness so you know if anything is missing, and then learns from itself and gets better over time. So you can see here it's now using the front-end developer agent to build out this project. And you can see it's just beginning to code that out directly here. And there's also four depth levels here as well. So you've got um quick, standard, everyday research, deep, and exhaustive. Right? So exhaustive is like over 40 minutes of um academic level deep research. Pretty insane. And then you can just use that example to to start going with this. There's also a bunch of um MCP servers that you can add. So for example like Tavi context 7 um Serena playright magic which autogenerates UI components morphm so it's context aware and chrome dev tools for performance analysis and debugging so if we have a look inside cl code you can see that's beginning to work out right now um it was pretty simple and easy to set up um the main thing to note here is like before super clawed or without super clawude claw code works but it's generic no structure no shortcuts no experts no memory um and you write long process every single time. Now you might be asking, okay, what is the agency like? What what's all this about? You know, the agency agents uh GitHub project. Well, basically think of it like this, right? Imagine walking into a building um and picking from like 61 different experts. for example, like a front-end developer, a brand designer, a Reddit marketing specialist, right? Each one knows exactly what they're doing. Each one has its own unique personality and style. And each one gives you like real actionable deliverables, right? And so that's what the agency is. So it's a collection of 61 agent files you can plug into tools like Cloud Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Gemini, CLI, and more. And when you load one of these agents, your AI tool literally transforms into a specialist. So it's not like at like a developer. It's a deeply crafted personality with workflows, success metrics, um, code examples, and a specific communication style. Now, one thing I want to say before you install anything like this is I always check the skillmd files. You just want to be careful before you install anything external. I do it for fun to show you as experiments on YouTube. But if you're not sure about installing any of this or you don't want to do it, etc., don't install it. Like, just watch my videos and learn from it. And also check the skillmd files. You can run them through claude as well. You could even rewrite them yourself and then install them yourself instead. But the main thing is like just be careful when you do install skills to something like claw code. So you've got engineering, design, marketing, products, project management, uh testing, support, spatial computing, and specialized. You can set it up literally in one click, as I've done today. It's open source as well, so you can use it however you want. It has 12,000 stars on GitHub. I honestly don't pay too much. New Claude code update is insane. So Claude can now work while you sleep. No, I'm not joking. Anthropic just dropped an update that turns Claude into a robot that codes on a timer. You set it once, walk away, and it just keeps going. Today I'm going to show you exactly how it works, how to set it up in like 2 minutes, and the real use cases that are going to blow your mind. This is the biggest clawed update yet, and nobody is talking about it. So, here's the problem. Every AI tool right now works the same way. You type something, it does something, you type again, it does something again. It's like having an employee who only works when you tap them on the shoulder every single time. That gets old fast, especially if you're a developer or you run a team or you just have stuff that needs to happen every single day without you thinking about it. What if I told you that's over now? Claude Code just launched something called scheduled tasks and it changes everything. You give Claude a job. You tell it when to do that job and then Claude just does it every hour, every morning, every Monday, whatever you want. You set it up once and it runs on autopilot. Think about that for a second. That's not an assistant anymore. That's a worker. That's an autonomous agent sitting on your computer doing real work while you go live your life. Let me show you how this actually works. It's stupid simple. You open Claude Code on your desktop. You click schedule in the sidebar. You hit new task. Then you fill in four things. A name, a description, the prompt you want it to run, and how often you want it to run. That's it. You click run now to test it. And boom, it shows up in your scheduled sessions panel. Done. Now, here's the part that gets really fun. You don't even need to write fancy code to schedule things. You can literally type in plain English something like run my tests every six hours or check for bugs every morning at 9. Claude understands that. It just works and when the time comes, here's what happens behind the scenes. The desktop app checks the schedule every minute. When it's go time, Claude launches a brand new session. It runs your prompt and then it sends you a notification with the results. Every single run is a full Claude code session. That means Claude can edit files, run commands, create commits, open pull requests, even use MCP tools and APIs. This is not some watered down version. This is the full thing running on a clock. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every single comment, so make sure you drop one below. All right, let me walk you through the scheduling options because they're really flexible. You've got manual, which means it only runs when you click it. Hourly, which runs every hour. Daily, which runs at a specific time, weekdays, which runs Monday through Friday. And weekly, which runs on a specific day and time. So, if you want Claude to check your dependencies every Monday morning, done. If you want it to run tests at midnight every night, done. If you want a morning briefing at 8:00 a.m. on weekdays, done. You just tell it what to do and when. Now, let me give you the use cases because this is where it gets insane. Number one, automated bug fixing. You set Claude to scan your error logs every 4 hours. If it finds recurring errors, it figures out what's wrong. Then, it creates a branch, writes the fix, and opens a pull request or while you're eating lunch or sleeping or doing literally anything else. Number two, automated PR review. Every morning, Claude reviews all the new pull requests in your repo. It reads the code, spots problems, leaves comments. Before your team even opens their laptops, Claude has already done the first pass. Number three, dependency updates. Once a week, Claude checks your packages, finds anything outdated, updates them, and creates a pull request. No more forgetting to update stuff for 6 months and then everything breaks. Number four, morning developer briefings. Every weekday at 8 a.m. Claude pulls together all the new GitHub issues, all the open pull requests, all the failing tests, and it creates a nice clean report so you know exactly what's going on before your first sip of coffee. Number five, AI DevOps agent. Claude monitors your server logs, test results, and deployments, and it responds automatically. That's not a tool anymore. That's a team member. Now, if you want to go even deeper on stuff like this and learn how to actually use AI to scale your business and automate your workflows, check out the AI profit boardroom. It's the best place to learn how to use tools like Claude Code to get more customers and save hundreds of hours with AI automation. Link is in the description and comments. All right, let me give you some actual prompts you can copy and use right now. Prompt one, daily code review. You type this every weekday at 9:00 a.m. Review the latest commits in this repository. Identify potential bugs or performance issues. Write a summary report and suggest improvements. That's it. Claude handles the rest. Prompt to automated bug fixing. Every 3 hours. Scan the error logs in / logs. If any recurring errors appear, identify the root cause and attempt to fix them. If a fix is possible, create a branch and open a pull request. Imagine waking up and your bugs are already fixed. That's what this does. Prompt three, morning developer briefing. Every weekday at 8 a.m., generate a summary of new GitHub issues. Open pull requests and failing tests. Create a developer briefing report. Your entire team starts the day knowing exactly what matters. Prompt for test automation every 6 hours. Run the test suite if any tests fail. Diagnose the issue and propose a fix. Claude becomes your QA department on autopilot. Now, here's a few things you need to know before you go set this up because there are some limitations and I want to be straight with you about them. First, your computer has to be awake. If your machine goes to sleep, the task gets skipped. But there's a nice little safety net. When your computer wakes back up, Claude can run one catchup task to cover what it missed. And Anthropic also added an option where Claude can keep your computer awake to finish scheduled runs. So, that's cool. Second, the desktop app has to be open. This is not cloud automation. Everything runs locally on your machine. So if you close the app, nothing happens. Third, each run counts against your usage. Every scheduled run is treated like a full clawed code session. So keep that in mind when you're setting stuff to run every hour. Fourth, this works on Mac OS and Windows right now. Linux support for the desktop scheduled tasks is not available yet. Now, where does Claude actually store these tasks? Every scheduled task creates a folder on your machine. It goes in a hidden claw directory under scheduled tasks with the task name. Inside that folder, there's a file called skill.md. That file has your task name, description, and prompt. If you want to tweak what Claude does on the next run, you just edit that file. Super clean, super simple. Here's why I think this matters more than people realize. AI has been reactive since the beginning. You ask, it answers. That's been the entire model. This update breaks that model. Claude doesn't wait for you anymore. It just works on a schedule in the background doing real work. That's not an assistant. That's an autonomous developer. And we're only at the beginning of this right now. It's scheduled tasks on your desktop. But think about where this goes next. Cloud tasks, multi- aent coordination, AI teams that run projects without any human input at all. This is a huge shift. And if you're not paying attention to this stuff, you're going to fall behind fast. So, let me recap. Claude Code now has scheduled tasks. You set a prompt. You set a schedule, Claude runs it automatically. It can fix bugs, review code, update packages, write reports, monitor systems, all on autopilot. From there, what we also want to do is make sure that we have persistent memory set up. This allows clawed code to remember every session before, right? And the reason for that is like if you set up a project and then you want to run a new one, well, it's going to be super annoying if it doesn't remember anything that you worked on previously cuz you would have to load in the old project, which takes up more tokens, consumes your time, and makes you less productive. So today we are going to be looking at how to use agent teams inside of Clawude Code. So if you've never used claw code before, you can now actually use agent teams directly inside here to get started. We're going to get started on setting this up so that we can use agent teams to improve our projects. Now the main benefit of this is that you can have team of multiple agents coordinating and coding in parallel so that you can get tasks done better and faster. All right, so for example, there's a big difference between sub agents and agent teams, right? They own a context window. They can actually message each other. You can have agents message each other directly, they can share the task list with self-coordinate co coordination and also you know if you've got like complex work that requires discussion and collaboration then agent teams are probably going to be better than using sub agents and also the token cost is higher of course. So to set this up we need to make sure that we've enabled um clawed agent experimental teams. So let's get started right here. I'm going to open up the terminal. Let's escape from this. Just going to make sure we've got an uh agent teams enabled. You see, it's actually looking at the agent teams documentation. And there's two ways to do this. You can enable them by setting the code experimental agent teams environment variable to either this in your shell environment also through your settings in JSON. All right. So, you see we've enabled agent teams here. Now, one thing that I've also added is fast mode. Fast mode was literally just announced a few hours ago. It does cost six times more tokens than normal. So, it's great for me to show you on a live stream. Wouldn't recommend enabling that normally. Um, unless you are really careful about your time and also you want to focus on productivity instead. All right. So, we're going to allow this to edit the settings. So, it can edit its own settings in cycl code here. And then let's say for example you've got a website like for example we actually built out a new version of the AR profit border website which is my AI automation community and we've deployed that using this chat right here right so if we go to this link we have this website deployed from claw code right and this was built using claude opus 4.6 six and fast mode inside claw code. Right. So what we're going to do now is we're going to use agent teams to improve this project. All right. So you see it says once uh once we updated the settings it says done. Agent teams are now enabled in your cloud settings JSON. You will need to restart claw code for the change to take effect. Once restarted agent teams will let you coordinate multiple claw code sessions working together in parallel. Useful for complex tasks. Right. So, we're going to just restart Claw Code like so. We'll terminate this. Then, we'll open up this. Let's get Claw Code reinstalled. Now, it's starting up again and we'll say, okay, build out a new website for AI profit boardroom using agent teams. And we'll take the information for the AI profit boardroom here. Plug that in. Boom. There we go. All right. Now, we're also using fast mode with agent teams. So, you know, if you compare preopus 4.6 versus after opus 4.6, 6. A lot has changed. Number one, you've got a better API. Number two, you've got more context window with a million context window with Opus 4.6 whereas Opus 4.5 was just 200K token context window, right? Top of that, you get fast mode and you get agent teams. So, a lot has changed with claw code and it's crazy to see how much they innovated um with improvements like this. So, I'm excited to see what we get back on this particular project. And you can see that we're now building out a to-do list here. is going to create a team to build this out. Right? So you can see it says now let me set up the project directory and create a team to build this in parallel. Then it creates a task list and spawns some agents to work in parallel. Right? And we've got the team working on this project here. So this is the to-do list and agents work in parallel to work on this together. Right? So they're going to work on these three tasks first and then they're going to do this together at the end to deploy the site to production. Right. So you got one agent that can build the HTML structure for the AR profit boardroom community. You've got another that can build the CSS styles for the AR profit boardroom and then another that's going to add JavaScript interactivity. Claude Code just dropped something wild. Agent Swarm is here. Multiple AI agents working together like a real team. They talk to each other, share tasks, and coordinate automatically. This changes everything about AI automation. Let me show you how this actually works. So Claude Code just released Agent Swarm, and this is honestly one of the biggest updates I've seen in AI automation this year. We're talking about multiple clawed agents working together simultaneously on complex tasks, not just one agent doing one thing. A whole team of AI agents collaborating like actual employees. Now, before I dive into exactly how this works, let me explain why this matters. When you're running a business, you've got multiple things happening at once. You might need to analyze data while someone else writes content while another person builds out your systems. Usually, with AI tools, you're stuck doing one thing at a time, or you're manually coordinating between different tools. Agent Swarm changes that completely. You can now have one clawed agent handling your customer research, another one building your email sequences and a third one optimizing your landing pages, all at the same time, all coordinated, all working toward the same goal. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of SEO agency Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. Let me break down what Agent Swarm actually is. It's a system inside Claude Code that lets you run multiple Claude instances together. Each agent gets its own specific task. But here's where it gets interesting. These agents can communicate with each other. They can share information. They can pass tasks back and forth. And there's a central management system that keeps everything organized. Think of it like managing a real team, except your team works at AI speed and never needs sleep. Here's what makes this different from just opening multiple clawed tabs. With agent swarm, the agents are actually aware of each other. Agent one can send a message to agent two saying, "Hey, I finished the customer analysis. Here's what I found. Can you use this to write the email sequence?" And agent two receives that, processes it, and gets to work. You're not copying and pasting between windows. You're not manually coordinating. The system handles it. Now, let me show you a real example of how you'd use this for something like growing the AI profit boardroom. Let's say I want to create a complete customer acquisition system. I need market research, landing page copy, email sequences, and social media content. Normally, this would take me hours of back and forth with AI tools. With Agent Swarm, here's how it works. Agent one gets the task. Analyze our target audience for the AI profit boardroom. find their pain points, their goals, what keeps them up at night. This agent digs through data, looks at competitive communities, identifies exactly who we're serving. Agent two gets the task using the research from agent one, write compelling landing page copy that speaks directly to those pain points and shows how AI automation solves their problems. Agent three gets the task, create a five email welcome sequence that nurtures new members and gets them to take action immediately. Agent 4 gets the task, generates social media posts that highlight the community benefits and drive traffic to the landing page. Now, if you want to dive even deeper into AI automation, I've got something special for you. I run a community called the AI Profit Boardroom. The best place to scale your business, get more customers, and save hundreds with AI automation. Learn how to save time and automate your business with AI tools like Claude Code. The link is in the comments and description. All four agents start working simultaneously. Agent one finishes the research and automatically passes the insights to the other agents. They don't wait for you to manually transfer information. They just get it and keep moving. Agent two uses that research to write copy that actually resonates because it's based on real data about what our audience needs. Agent three builds emails that reference the same pain points and solutions, keeping everything consistent. Agent 4 creates posts that match the messaging across all the other content. And you're watching this happen in real time through the central management dashboard. Here's where it gets even better. You can give the entire swarm a highle goal and they'll figure out how to divide the work. You don't have to micromanage every single task. For example, you could tell the swarm, create a complete launch campaign for our new AI automation course in the AI profit boardroom. The swarm looks at that goal and automatically decides, okay, we need research, we need positioning, we need sales copy, we need email sequences, we need ads, we need social content. Then it assigns different agents to handle each piece. This is what I mean by coordination. The agents aren't just working in parallel. They're working together strategically. Now, let me talk about the technical setup because this is actually simpler than you might think. Claude Code runs in your terminal. You install it once and it integrates with your development environment. For Agent Swarm, you're basically telling Claude Code to spin up multiple instances instead of just one. You create a configuration file that defines what each agent does. You give each agent a name, a role, and specific instructions. Then you define how they communicate with each other. The syntax is straightforward. You're not writing complex code. You're essentially describing your team structure in plain language. Here's a practical example of how you'd set this up for the AI profit boardroom. Agent name, research agent, role, audience analysis. Task: Identify pain points and opportunities for AI automation in small businesses. Agent name, content agent, role, copywriting. Task, create persuasive content that converts visitors into community members. Agent name, technical agent, role implementation. task. Build landing pages, setup automation, handle technical execution, agent name, strategy, agent, role, coordination, task, oversee the entire process, and make sure everything aligns with our growth goals. You save that configuration, run one command, and boom, your agent swarm starts working. The inter agent messaging is where things get really powerful. Let's say the research agent discovers that our target audience is struggling with manual data entry and wants to automate their workflows. That agent sends a message to the content agent with that specific insight. The content agent receives it and immediately adjusts the landing page copy to emphasize how the AI profit boardroom teaches workflow automation that eliminates manual data entry. The technical agent gets looped in and makes sure the landing page has examples of workflow automation front and center. The strategy agent monitors everything and makes sure the messaging is consistent across all touch points. This happens automatically. You're not playing traffic cop between different AI conversations. Now, here's something most people miss about Agent Swarm. It's not just about speed, it's about quality. When you have specialized agents focused on specific tasks, they get better at those tasks. The research agent isn't trying to also write copy and build pages. It's laser focused on research. So, it goes deeper, finds better insights, and delivers higher quality analysis. Same with the content agent. It's not distracted by technical implementation or strategy. is just writing the best possible copy based on the research it receives. This specialization means each piece of your project gets expert level attention. And because the agents share information automatically, there's no loss of context. Everything stays connected. Now, let's talk about the centralized management system because this is what keeps everything from becoming chaos. The management system gives you a dashboard where you can see what each agent is doing, what tasks are complete, what's in progress, and where there might be issues. You can pause agents, reassign tasks, or jump in and give new instructions without stopping the entire swarm. If you notice the content agent is creating copy that's a bit off tone, you can give it specific feedback, and it adjusts immediately while the other agents keep working on their tasks. This level of control means you're never locked into whatever the AI decides. You're orchestrating a team, not just watching automation run wild. Now, if you want to dive even deeper into AI automation, I've got something special for you. I run a community called the AI Profit Boardroom. New Claude Code agent update is insane. Claude Code just dropped something absolutely wild. Agent teams, multiple AI agents working together like a real dev team. This is not a drill. Your workflow is about to get supercharged. Let me show you why this changes everything. So, Anthropic just released agent teams for clawed code. And I'm not exaggerating when I say this is absolutely insane. We're talking multiple AI agents working together on the same project. They communicate with each other. They share tasks. They solve problems independently. This is like having an entire development team, but it's all AI. Let me explain what this actually means. Before this update, you had Claude Code working solo. It was powerful, sure. But now, now you can spawn an entire team of agents. A lead agent manages everything. It creates teammates. It assigns tasks. It coordinates the whole operation. Each agent has its own context and can work independently while still communicating with the team. This is a complete gamecher for complex projects. Here's what makes this different from regular sub aents. Sub aents are like assistants. They do one thing and report back. Agent teams are different. They're independent. They can message each other directly. They share a task list. They handle dependencies automatically. One agent can be working on the front end while another debugs the back end while a third reviews security all at the same time all coordinated. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of SEO agency Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. Think about what this means for your business. Let's say you're building a new landing page for the AI profit boardroom. Instead of one agent doing everything, you spawn a team. One agent focuses on conversion optimization. Another handles the design and layout. A third works on the copy and messaging. A fourth checks mobile responsiveness. They all work together. They communicate. They solve problems faster than any single agent could. The way it works is actually pretty simple. You enable it with one environment variable. Then you just prompt the lead agent. Like you're talking to a project manager. You say something like create a team to build an email automation system for AI profit boardroom members. We need someone on workflow design, someone on email templates, and someone on testing. The lead agent immediately spawns the team and starts coordinating. Each agent gets assigned tasks. The tasks have dependencies. So if one agent needs to finish something before another can start, the system handles that automatically. You can watch it all happen in real time. There's two ways to view it. You can use in process mode where you switch between agents with keyboard shortcuts or you can use split panes with T-Mox or iter 2 and see all the agents working at once. It's like watching a dev team collaborate but it's all happening on your screen. Let me walk you through a real example. Say you want to create a comprehensive onboarding system for new AI profit boardroom members. This is complex. You need welcome emails, tutorial videos, resource documents, and a tracking system. That's a lot for one agent. But with teams, you prompt the lead. Build a complete onboarding system for AI profit boardroom. Spawn a team with specialists in email sequences, content creation, automation workflows, and analytics tracking. Boom. The lead creates four agents. The email specialist starts building the welcome sequence. The content creator works on the tutorial outline. The automation expert sets up the workflows. The analytics person builds the tracking dashboard. They're all working simultaneously. And here's the crazy part. They're messaging each other. The email specialist asks the content creator what topics to cover. The automation expert checks with analytics about what to track. It's real collaboration. And speaking of collaboration, if you want to learn how to save time and automate your entire business with AI tools like Claude Code Agent Teams, you need to check out the AI profit boardroom. This is where we teach you exactly how to implement these cuttingedge AI systems into your business. No fluff, just practical automation that actually works. We're using these exact tools to scale our operations and you can too. Link is in the description. Now, let's get back to these agent teams because it gets even better. The task management system is brilliant. Every task goes through three states: pending, in progress, completed. The lead agent can assign tasks or teammates can claim them themselves. Dependencies are automatic. If task B needs task A to finish first, the system won't let anyone start task B until task A is done. No confusion, no wasted effort, just pure efficiency. You can switch between agents using shift plus up or down arrows. So if you want to see what the email specialist is doing, you switch to them. You want to check on the automation expert switch. Again, it's seamless. And if you're using split panes, you don't even need to switch. You just look at the different panes and see everyone working at once. Now, let's talk about real use cases because this is where it gets practical. Imagine you're debugging a complex issue with your AI automation system. Instead of one agent trying everything, you create a team with three different debugging approaches. One agent investigates the data flow. Another checks the API connections. A third examines the error logs. They work in parallel. They share findings through messages. You find the bug three times faster. Or let's say you're building a new feature for your content generation system. You want to make sure it's perfect. You create a team with a developer, a security specialist, and a performance optimizer. The developer builds it. The security specialist checks for vulnerabilities. The performance optimizer makes sure it runs fast, all happening at the same time, all coordinated. The final product is way better than what one agent could build alone. Code reviews are another perfect use case. You have one agent review for security issues. Another agent reviews for performance bottlenecks. A third agent checks code quality and best practices. They each bring different perspectives. They each catch different issues. Your code quality goes through the roof. Here's what I love about this for business automation. Let's say you're creating a complete lead generation system for the AI profit boardroom. This involves landing pages, email sequences, chat bots, analytics, and integration with your CRM. That's massive. One agent would take forever and probably miss things. But with a team, you assign each component to a specialist. The landing page agent focuses on conversion. The email agent crafts sequences that nurture leads. The chatbot agent creates conversational flows. The analytics agent sets up tracking. The integration agent connects everything to your CRM. It all gets done faster and better. The agents communicate through a mailbox system, they can send direct messages to each other. So, if the landing page agent needs to know what fields the CRM requires, it messages the integration agent. If the email agent wants to coordinate with the chatbot agent on messaging consistency, they message directly. It's like Slack for AI agents and it works incredibly well. Now, I need to be real with you about some limitations because this is still experimental. First, it uses more tokens. Each agent has its own context. They're all running simultaneously. That means more API calls, more tokens consumed. It's more expensive than using a single agent. But for complex projects, it's worth it because of the time saved and quality gained. Second, the T-Mux or ITM2 setup can be a bit tricky if you're not familiar with terminal multipplexing. The inprocess mode is easier, but you can't see all agents at once. The split pane mode is amazing for visibility, but requires some setup. Not a dealbreaker, just something to know. Third, this is experimental. That means it might have bugs, it might change. Anthropic is still testing and improving it, but from what I've tested, it's already incredibly powerful, and it's only going to get better. Here are my best practices after testing this extensively. First, be specific with your initial prompt to the lead agent. Tell it exactly what kind of team you need and what each specialist should focus on. The more specific you are, the better the team performs. Second, use delegate mode. Hit shift plus tab and let the agents work autonomously. They'll claim tasks and coordinate without you micromanaging. Third, plan approval is your friend. Have the lead agent create a plan first. Review it. Make sure the team structure makes sense, then approve and let them execute. Fourth, keep task sizes reasonable. Don't create tasks that are too big or too vague. Break things down. The agents work best with clear, specific tasks. Fifth, use the messaging system. Encourage agents to communicate. That's where the magic happens. When they share context and coordinate, the results are way better. And if you're serious about AI automation, you need to jump on this now. Check out the AI success lab for the complete breakdown and community support. I check out the AI profit boardroom for the full training. And most importantly, go test agent teams yourself. Build something, break something, learn, iterate. That's how you master this stuff. Thanks for watching. Hit subscribe if you want more AI updates like this. Claude Code can now make videos, real videos, professional videos, the kind you'd normally spend hours building in Premiere or Final Cut, except you don't touch an editor. You just type what you want and Claude builds it. I'm talking animations, transitions, text effects, audio, everything. This is Remotion's new agent skills integration, and it just dropped in January 2026. Here's why this matters. Most AI video tools give you templates. You pick a style, you swap some text, you get generic content. But with Remotion and Claude Code, you get pixel level control. You can build custom animations. You can sequence clips exactly how you want. You can add your own fonts, colors, brand elements. It's like having a video developer on demand. And the best part, you control it all with simple prompts. No code, no technical knowledge. Just tell Claude what you want. Let me show you how this works. First, you need to install the remote skill. Open your terminal and run this command. MPX skills add dev skills. Skill remotion. That's it. One command. Now, Claw Code has all the knowledge it needs about motion. It knows how to build animations. It knows how to sequence clips. It knows how to handle assets and transitions. Next, create a new Remotion project. Run MPX create video latest. Install the dependencies with MPM install. And now you're ready to build. Here's where it gets fun. You can prompt Claude Code to create entire video compositions. Let's say you want a promotional video for the AI Profit Boardroom. You could say something like this. Create a 30-cond explainer video for the AI profit boardroom. Show animated text highlighting our AI automation community. Add smooth transitions between scenes. Include benefits like save time, automate tasks, grow your business, make it professional and modern. Claude Code takes that prompt and builds the entire video structure. It writes the React code. It sets up the animations. It handles the timing. It creates the composition. And you can preview it instantly with MPX Remotion Preview. If you want changes, just tell Claude, "Make the text fade in slower. Add a 3D transition between the first and second scene. Change the background color to match our brand." Claude updates the code in real time. When you're happy with it, render the final video. Run MPX Remotion Render, and you get a highquality MP4 file ready to upload, ready to share, ready to convert. You can also see that at the bottom it will auto compact inside the chat. So one of the good things about claw code now is like previously it would have a low context window which meant that it constantly ran out of tokens right so you could only talk so much inside the chat before it just shut down and wouldn't respond right because you ran out of context window now it has this autocompat feature and basically what that means is even if it hits the context window even if you run out of tokens and you can't you go over 200,000 tokens which is the token limit for claude then it will autoco compact inside the chat so you avoid that problem and you can see now the page here has opened. So the code is often cleaner than what like a junior developer would write and you can fix issues in seconds not hours. Right? Other people say this only works for simple stuff. Right? Vibe comb plus cloud code is insane. Hey, today I'm going to show you something that's going to completely change how you build stuff with AI. I'm talking about Vibe Canban plus Cloud Code working together. This is big cuz you can now run multiple coding agents at the same time without them breaking each other's work. No more babysitting terminal logs. You can actually focus on planning and reviewing while AI does the heavy lifting in the background. This is the future of building with AI and it's available right now. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of SEO agency Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. Let me break down what Vibe Cambban actually is and why it matters. Vibe Camb is a tool that lets you manage multiple coding agents at once. Think of it like having a team of developers working on different parts of your project at the same time. But here's the crazy part. They don't step on each other's toes. They don't create conflicts. They just work in parallel while you focus on the bigger picture. Before this, if you wanted to use clawed code or any coding agent, you had to watch it work. You had to sit there looking at terminal logs. You had to make sure it didn't mess something up. It was like micromanaging a developer, except the developer was an AI. That's not efficient. That's not how you scale. That's not how you build a business that runs without you being stuck in the weeds. And with Vibe Camban, everything changes. You can set up multiple tasks. Each task gets its own coding agent and they all work at the same time. While they're coding in the background, you're doing the highlevel stuff. You're planning the next feature. You're reviewing the work that's already done. You're thinking about strategy instead of syntax. Let me give you a real example of how this works for the AI profit boardroom. Let's say we need to build out three different features for our community platform. We need a new landing page that converts visitors into members. We need an automated email sequence that onboards new members. And we need a dashboard that shows members their progress with AI automation. Before Vibe Comban, I'd have to do these one by one with cloud code. I'd start the landing page, wait for it to finish, check it, then move to the email sequence, wait, check, then the dashboard, wait, check. That's hours of just waiting and watching. Vibe Kambban. I set up three separate tasks in the Kambban board. One task for the landing page, one for the email sequence, one for the dashboard. I assign each task to a claude code agent. And I hit go. All three agents start working at the same time. The landing page agent is writing HTML and CSS for a high converting page that explains the value of AI automation. The email sequence agent is building out the automation that welcomes new members and shows them how to get started. The dashboard agent is creating the interface that tracks member progress and engagement. Now, if you want to dive even deeper into AI automation, I've got something special for you. I run a community called the AI Profit Boardroom. The best place to scale your business, get more customers, and save hundreds with AI automation. Learn how to save time and automate your business with AI tools like Vibe, Cambban, and Claude Code. The link is in the comments and description. It's at school.com/iprofitlab. And what am I doing while this happens? I'm not watching logs. I'm planning the next set of features. I'm thinking about what else we need to make the AI profit boardroom even better. I'm reviewing the work as it comes in through vibe camb tool. This is the key. The diff tool shows me exactly what changed. I can see what the agent built. I can approve it or ask for changes. And I do this at my own pace, not at the pace of the terminal scrolling by. Here's why the diff tool is so important. Like when you're building with AI, you need to review the code. You need to make sure it actually does what you want. But if you're watching terminal logs, you're trying to review code as it's being written. That's impossible. It's like trying to proofread a book while someone's still typing it. With Vibe Camb's diff tool, you review after the work is done. You see a clean before and after. You can take your time. You can think about whether this is the right solution, and you can give feedback that actually improves the next iteration. Let me walk you through the actual workflow. First, you open Vibe Cambban. You see your camb board with columns like to-do, in progress, review, and done. You create a new task card. Let's say the task is build a landing page for AI profit boardroom that converts visitors by showing the amazing value of AI automation and explains all the benefits of our community. You write that in the task description. Then you assign it to a claw code agent. The agent picks up the task and moves it to in progress. Now, here's where it gets good. You can create another task right away. Build an automated email sequence that onboards new AI profit boardroom members and teaches them the basics of AI automation. Assign that to another Claude code agent and another task. Create a member dashboard for AI profit boardroom that shows progress, community activity, and upcoming events. Assign that to a third agent. All three tasks are now in progress at the same time. All three agents are coding in parallel. You're not watching any of them work. You're doing other stuff. Maybe you're creating content. Maybe you're talking to members. Maybe you're planning the next phase of your business. The agents work in the background. And when they're done, the task move to the review column. That's your queue. You open the diff tool. You see what the first agent built for the landing page. You review the HTML. You check the copy. You make sure it actually sells the value of AI automation. If it looks good, you approve it and move the task to done. If it needs changes, you add comments and send it back. Same process for the other tasks. Review the email sequence. Review the dashboard. approve or request changes. And here's the thing, while you're reviewing one task, the other agents can still be working on their tasks. Or if you send something back for revisions, that agent can work on the fixes while you review something else. Everything happens in parallel. Nothing is blocked waiting for you to watch a terminal. This is how you scale with AI. You don't scale by doing more tasks yourself. You scale by managing more agents, doing more tasks at the same time. And Vibe Campban makes that possible. It's like going from being a solo developer to managing a team of developers, except the team is AI agents and they work way faster than humans. Now, let's talk about what this means for growing a business like the AI profit boardroom. When you can build faster, you can test more ideas. When you can test more ideas, you find what works. When you find what works, you scale it. That's the whole game. But most people get stuck at the building part. They have great ideas, but building takes too long or building takes too much focus or they don't know how to code, so they can't build at all. Vibe Camban plus Claude Code solves all of that. You don't need to know how to code. You just need to know what you want to build. You describe it in a task. You let the agent build it. You review it. You ship it. Then you move to the next idea. Instead of spending weeks building one feature, you can build multiple features in days. Instead of being the bottleneck, you become the strategic layer that decides what to build and whether it's good enough. Let me give you another example. Let's say you want to add a new feature to the AI profit boardroom. You want a tool that helps members generate content ideas for their business using AI. You could sit down and try to code this yourself or you could hire a developer or you could use Claude code and watch it build the whole thing while you stare at logs for an hour. Or you could use Vibe Camban. You create a task. Build a content idea generator for AI profit boardroom members that uses AI to suggest blog posts, social media content, and video topics based on their business niche. You assign it to an agent. You go do something else. An hour later, you come back. The task is in review. You check the diff. The agent built a clean interface where members enter their niche. The AI suggests 20 content ideas. Each idea has a title, a brief outline, and a hook. You test it. It works. You approve it. You ship it. total time you spent actively working on this maybe 15 minutes time the agent spent building an hour but you weren't watching that hour you were doing other high value work now multiply that across every feature you want to build every improvement you want to make every test you want to run that's the power of vibe comban you're not limited by how much you can personally code or watch being coded you're limited by how many good ideas you have and how fast you can review the work and reviewing is way faster than building here's another huge benefit vibe combat and keeps everything organized. When you're running multiple agents, it's easy to lose track of what's being built, what's done, what needs review, what's still in progress. The KBAN board solves that. You see everything at a glance. You know exactly what stage each task is in. You know what needs your attention next. You're not juggling terminal windows. You're not trying to remember which agent is working on which feature. It's all visual. It's all clear. And because everything goes through the diff tool for review, you maintain quality. You're not just blindly shipping whatever the agent builds. You're checking it. You're making sure it meets your standards. You're making sure it actually solves the problem you wanted to solve. This is critical. AI is powerful, but it's not perfect. Sometimes it misunderstands what you want. Sometimes it builds something that technically works but isn't quite right. The review step catches that. And because you're reviewing work that's already done instead of work that's happening in real time, you can actually give thoughtful feedback. The old way was like you have an old idea for a business tool, you write a specification document, you find a developer to build it, you wait weeks and months for delivery, it gets expensive, then every change gets more expensive and then half of what gets built isn't actually what you wanted and you eventually end up giving up on custom tools and using generic software, right? That was the old way before anti-gravity. People say AI coding skills are too bulky and unreliable, right?
